mn63:1.2: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn63:10.1: And why, Mālukyaputta, has this been declared by me?
mn63:10.2: Because, Mālukyaputta, it is beneficial, fundamental to the spiritual life, and leads to disenchantment, fading away, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, full awakening, and *nibbāna*.
mn63:10.3: That is why it has been declared by me.
mn63:10.4: Therefore, Mālukyaputta, remember as undeclared what has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:10.5: and remember as declared what has been declared by me.”
mn63:10.7: Satisfied, Venerable Mālukyaputta delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn63:10.8: The Shorter Discourse to Mālukyaputta is finished, the third.
mn63:2.1: Then, while Venerable Mālukyaputta was alone in seclusion, this reflection arose in his mind:
mn63:2.10: then I will live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā*;
mn63:2.11: but if the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me:
mn63:2.12: ‘The world is eternal’ or ‘The world is not eternal’ …
mn63:2.13: or ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:2.14: then I will renounce the training and return to a lower life.”
mn63:2.2: “These speculative views that have been left undeclared, set aside, and rejected by the *Bhagavā*:
mn63:2.3: ‘The world is eternal,’ ‘The world is not eternal,’ ‘The world is finite,’ ‘The world is infinite,’ ‘The soul is the same as the body,’ ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ ‘The Tathāgata exists after death,’ ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death,’ ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death,’ ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:2.4: these the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me.
mn63:2.5: What the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me does not please me; I do not accept it.
mn63:2.6: So I will go to the *Bhagavā* and ask him about this matter.
mn63:2.8: ‘The world is eternal’ or ‘The world is not eternal’ …
mn63:2.9: or ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:3.1: Then in the evening, Venerable Mālukyaputta rose from seclusion and went to the *Bhagavā*. After going to the *Bhagavā* and paying homage to him, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, Venerable Mālukyaputta said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn63:3.10: ‘The world is eternal’ or ‘The world is not eternal’ …
mn63:3.11: or ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:3.12: then I will live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā*.
mn63:3.13: But if the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me:
mn63:3.14: ‘The world is eternal’ or ‘The world is not eternal’ …
mn63:3.15: or ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:3.16: then I will renounce the training and return to a lower life.
mn63:3.18: ‘The world is eternal,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The world is eternal.’
mn63:3.2: “Here, bhante, while I was alone in seclusion, this reflection arose in my mind—
mn63:3.20: ‘The world is not eternal,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The world is not eternal.’
mn63:3.21: But if the *Bhagavā* does not know:
mn63:3.22: ‘The world is eternal’ or ‘The world is not eternal,’ then for one who does not know and does not see, the straightforward thing is just this:
mn63:3.23: ‘I do not know, I do not see.’
mn63:3.25: ‘The world is finite,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The world is finite’;
mn63:3.27: ‘The world is infinite,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The world is infinite.’
mn63:3.28: But if the *Bhagavā* does not know:
mn63:3.29: ‘The world is finite’ or ‘The world is infinite,’ then for one who does not know and does not see, the straightforward thing is just this:
mn63:3.3: these speculative views that have been left undeclared, set aside, and rejected by the *Bhagavā*:
mn63:3.30: ‘I do not know, I do not see.’
mn63:3.32: ‘The soul is the same as the body,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The soul is the same as the body’;
mn63:3.34: ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The soul is one thing and the body another.’
mn63:3.35: But if the *Bhagavā* does not know:
mn63:3.36: ‘The soul is the same as the body’ or ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ then for one who does not know and does not see, the straightforward thing is just this:
mn63:3.37: ‘I do not know, I do not see.’
mn63:3.39: ‘The Tathāgata exists after death,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The Tathāgata exists after death’;
mn63:3.4: ‘The world is eternal,’ ‘The world is not eternal’ …
mn63:3.41: ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death.’
mn63:3.42: But if the *Bhagavā* does not know:
mn63:3.43: ‘The Tathāgata exists after death’ or ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death,’ then for one who does not know and does not see, the straightforward thing is just this:
mn63:3.44: ‘I do not know, I do not see.’
mn63:3.46: ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death’;
mn63:3.48: ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death,’ let the *Bhagavā* declare to me, ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death.’
mn63:3.49: But if the *Bhagavā* does not know:
mn63:3.5: ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:3.50: ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death’ or ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death,’ then for one who does not know and does not see, the straightforward thing is just this:
mn63:3.51: ‘I do not know, I do not see.’”
mn63:3.6: these the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me.
mn63:3.7: What the *Bhagavā* does not declare to me does not please me; I do not accept it.
mn63:3.8: So I will go to the *Bhagavā* and ask him about this matter.
mn63:4.1: “What do you think, Mālukyaputta? Did I ever say this:
mn63:4.10: ‘Come, Mālukyaputta, live the spiritual life under me, and I will declare to you:
mn63:4.11: “The world is eternal” or “The world is not eternal” …
mn63:4.12: “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’;
mn63:4.13: and you do not say to me:
mn63:4.14: ‘I, bhante, will live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* will declare to me:
mn63:4.15: “The world is eternal” or “The world is not eternal” …
mn63:4.16: “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’.
mn63:4.17: That being so, foolish man, who are you accusing of what?
mn63:4.2: ‘Come, Mālukyaputta, live the spiritual life under me, and I will declare to you:
mn63:4.3: “The world is eternal,” or “The world is not eternal,” or “The world is finite,” or “The world is infinite,” or “The soul is the same as the body,” or “The soul is one thing and the body another,” or “The Tathāgata exists after death,” or “The Tathāgata does not exist after death,” or “The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death,” or “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’?”
mn63:4.5: “Or did you ever say this to me:
mn63:4.6: ‘I, bhante, will live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* will declare to me:
mn63:4.7: “The world is eternal,” or “The world is not eternal,” or “The world is finite,” or “The world is infinite,” or “The soul is the same as the body,” or “The soul is one thing and the body another,” or “The Tathāgata exists after death,” or “The Tathāgata does not exist after death,” or “The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death,” or “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’?”
mn63:4.9: “So then, Mālukyaputta, I do not say to you:
mn63:5.11: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the man by whom I was struck—what his name and clan are.’
mn63:5.13: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the man by whom I was struck—whether he is tall, short, or of medium height.’
mn63:5.15: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the man by whom I was struck—whether he is dark, brown, or tawny-skinned.’
mn63:5.17: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the man by whom I was struck—whether he comes from such-and-such a village, town, or city.’
mn63:5.19: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the bow by which I was struck—whether it was a bow or a longbow.’
mn63:5.2: ‘I will not live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā* until the *Bhagavā* declares to me:
mn63:5.21: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the bowstring by which I was struck—whether it was made of arka fiber, hemp, sinew, murva fiber, or milkweed.’
mn63:5.23: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the shaft by which I was struck—whether it was from a wild-growing reed or a cultivated one.’
mn63:5.25: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the shaft by which I was struck—whether it was fletched with the feathers of a vulture, a heron, a hawk, a peacock, or a loose-jawed bird.’
mn63:5.27: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the shaft by which I was struck—whether it was bound with sinew from a cow, a buffalo, an antelope, or a monkey.’
mn63:5.29: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the arrow by which I was struck—whether it was an ordinary arrow, a razor-head, a barbed head, an iron arrow, a calf-tooth head, or an oleander-leaf head.’—
mn63:5.3: “The world is eternal” or “The world is not eternal” …
mn63:5.30: before that was known, Mālukyaputta, that man would die.
mn63:5.31: In the same way, Mālukyaputta, whoever might say this:
mn63:5.32: ‘I will not live the spiritual life under the *Bhagavā* until the *Bhagavā* declares to me:
mn63:5.33: “The world is eternal” or “The world is not eternal” …
mn63:5.34: “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’—
mn63:5.35: that would still remain undeclared by the Tathāgata, Mālukyaputta, and meanwhile that person would die.
mn63:5.4: “The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death”’—
mn63:5.5: that would still remain undeclared by the Tathāgata, Mālukyaputta, and meanwhile that person would die.
mn63:5.6: Suppose, Mālukyaputta, a man were struck by an arrow thickly smeared with poison.
mn63:5.7: His friends, companions, relatives, and kinsmen would provide a surgeon to remove the arrow.
mn63:5.9: ‘I will not have this arrow removed until I know the man by whom I was struck—whether he was a noble, a brahmin, a merchant, or a worker.’
mn63:6.1: ‘The world is eternal,’ Mālukyaputta: it is not the case that, if that view exists, the spiritual life could not be lived.
mn63:6.10: ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ Mālukyaputta: that too is not the case.
mn63:6.11: Whether there is the view ‘The soul is the same as the body’ or the view ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ there is still birth …
mn63:6.12: I make known the destruction in this very life.
mn63:6.13: ‘The Tathāgata exists after death,’ Mālukyaputta: it is not the case that, if that view exists, the spiritual life could not be lived.
mn63:6.14: ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death,’ Mālukyaputta: that too is not the case.
mn63:6.15: Whether there is the view ‘The Tathāgata exists after death’ or the view ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death,’ there is still birth …
mn63:6.16: for these I make known the destruction in this very life.
mn63:6.17: ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death,’ Mālukyaputta: it is not the case that, if that view exists, the spiritual life could not be lived.
mn63:6.18: ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death,’ Mālukyaputta: that too is not the case.
mn63:6.19: Whether there is the view ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death’ or the view ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death,’ there is still birth …
mn63:6.2: ‘The world is not eternal,’ Mālukyaputta: that too is not the case.
mn63:6.20: for these I make known the destruction in this very life.
mn63:6.3: Whether there is the view ‘The world is eternal’ or the view ‘The world is not eternal,’ there is still birth, there is aging, there is death, there are sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair;
mn63:6.4: for these I make known the destruction in this very life.
mn63:6.5: ‘The world is finite,’ Mālukyaputta: it is not the case that, if that view exists, the spiritual life could not be lived.
mn63:6.6: ‘The world is infinite,’ Mālukyaputta: that too is not the case.
mn63:6.7: Whether there is the view ‘The world is finite’ or the view ‘The world is infinite,’ there is still birth, there is aging, there is death, there are sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair;
mn63:6.8: for these I make known the destruction in this very life.
mn63:6.9: ‘The soul is the same as the body,’ Mālukyaputta: it is not the case that, if that view exists, the spiritual life could not be lived.
mn63:7.1: Therefore, Mālukyaputta, remember as undeclared what has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.10: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.11: ‘The soul is the same as the body’—
mn63:7.12: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.13: ‘The soul is one thing and the body another’—
mn63:7.14: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.16: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.17: ‘The Tathāgata does not exist after death’—
mn63:7.18: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.19: ‘The Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death’—
mn63:7.2: and remember as declared what has been declared by me.
mn63:7.20: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.21: ‘The Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’—
mn63:7.22: has been left undeclared by me.
mn63:7.3: And what, Mālukyaputta, has been left undeclared by me?
mn63:7.4: ‘The world is eternal,’ Mālukyaputta, has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.6: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:7.8: has been left undeclared by me;
mn63:8.1: And why, Mālukyaputta, has this been left undeclared by me?
mn63:8.2: Because, Mālukyaputta, it is not beneficial, not fundamental to the spiritual life, and does not lead to disenchantment, fading away, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, full awakening, or *nibbāna*.
mn63:8.3: That is why it has been left undeclared by me.
mn63:9.1: And what, Mālukyaputta, has been declared by me?
mn63:9.2: ‘This is *dukkha*,’ Mālukyaputta, has been declared by me;
mn63:9.7: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*’—
